pattern: Once you commit to processing group-one points one at a time, the only thing that matters about the past is which group-two points have already been touched -- so the remaining work collapses into a DP over that small bitmask of coverage.
hints: Think of it as: walk through the first group's points one at a time, deciding for each which of the second group's points it links to; the only state you need to remember afterward is the set of second-group points that have been linked to so far.
Precompute, for every point in the second group, the cheapest single edge that reaches it at all -- that lets you cheaply 'clean up' any second-group point nobody in the first group happened to pick.

Define dp(i, mask) as the minimum cost to have handled the first i points of group one, where mask records which group-two points are already connected; at i == size1, whatever bits are still 0 in mask must be paid for using the precomputed cheapest edge into each of those points.

edge_inputs.description: statement-style 2x3 grid with a shared cheap hub
size1 at its 12 maximum with size2 == 1
all zero costs -- everything is free, answer is 0
one group-two point is expensive from every group-one point except one, forcing that single edge to be reused instead of paid for twice
square grid where the diagonal is cheap and off-diagonal is expensive, tempting a greedy row-min approach to under-cover columns

stress.description: random rectangular cost grids up to the 12x12 constraint ceiling
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    size2 = rng.randint(1, min(12, max(1, size)))
    size1 = rng.randint(size2, 12)
    cost = [[rng.randint(0, 100) for _ in range(size2)] for _ in range(size1)]
    return [cost]
stress.note: size1 >= size2 enforced directly by construction
